questions.stem: A map $T : M \to M$ on a metric space is an isometry when it is invertible and satisfies which property?
options: $d(T(x), T(y)) = d(x, y)$ for all $x, y$.
$d(T(x), T(y)) \le d(x, y)$ for all $x, y$.
questions.explanation: An isometry preserves distances exactly: $d(T(x), T(y)) = d(x, y)$. The isometries of a metric space $M$ form a group under composition.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: By Proposition 2.2, every isometry $A$ of Euclidean space $E^N$ can be written in which form?
options: $A(x) = x + v$ for some vector $v$ only.
$A(x) = Bx + v$ with $B \in O(N)$ an orthogonal matrix and $v \in \mathbb{R}^N$.
questions.explanation: Every Euclidean isometry is affine: $A(x) = Bx + v$ where $B$ is orthogonal and $v$ is a translation vector. Conversely any such map is an isometry.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: By Lemma 2.1, a Euclidean isometry that fixes the origin must be which kind of map?
questions.explanation: Any isometry fixing the origin preserves the inner product (by the polarization identity) and is linear, hence it is an orthogonal linear map, an element of $O(N)$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A linear map $T$ on $\mathbb{R}^N$ is orthogonal precisely when its matrix $M$ satisfies which equation?
questions.explanation: Orthogonality means $T$ preserves the inner product, equivalent to $M^t M = I$. These matrices form the orthogonal group $O(N)$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The orientation preserving isometries of the Euclidean plane $E^2$ are exactly which maps?
options: The identity, translations, and rotations about a point.
questions.explanation: By Proposition 2.3 the orientation preserving isometries of $E^2$ are the identity, translations, and rotations about some point. Orientation reversing ones are reflections and glide reflections.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The orientation reversing isometries of the Euclidean plane $E^2$ are exactly which maps?
questions.explanation: An orientation reversing isometry of $E^2$ is either a reflection in a line, or a glide reflection (reflection in a line followed by a translation parallel to that line).
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A glide reflection of the Euclidean plane is which composite map?
options: A rotation followed by a translation toward the centre.
A reflection followed by a translation perpendicular to the mirror.
A reflection in a line $\ell$ followed by a translation parallel to $\ell$.
questions.explanation: A glide reflection reflects in a line $\ell$ and then translates parallel to $\ell$. The translation component along the mirror is what distinguishes it from an ordinary reflection.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For an isometry $A(x) = Bx + v$ of $E^2$, the determinant of the orthogonal part $B$ is $+1$ for which isometries?
options: The identity and rotations, which preserve orientation.
questions.explanation: An orthogonal $2 \times 2$ matrix has determinant $\pm 1$. It is $+1$ for the identity and rotations (orientation preserving) and $-1$ for reflections (orientation reversing).
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An isometry of $E^2$ that fixes the origin and has orthogonal part of determinant $-1$ is which kind of map?
options: A reflection in a line through the origin.
A rotation through a nonzero angle.
A glide reflection with nonzero glide.
questions.explanation: An origin-fixing isometry is an orthogonal map. Determinant $-1$ in $O(2)$ gives a reflection in a line through the origin; determinant $+1$ gives a rotation about the origin.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The composition of two reflections of $E^2$ in two parallel lines is which isometry?
options: A translation perpendicular to the lines by twice the distance between them.
questions.explanation: Reflecting in two parallel lines gives a translation in the direction perpendicular to both lines, by twice the distance separating them.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The composition of two reflections of $E^2$ in two lines meeting at a point $P$ at angle $\alpha$ is which isometry?
options: A reflection in the angle bisector.
A rotation about $P$ through twice the angle, $2\alpha$.
questions.explanation: Reflecting in two lines through $P$ gives a rotation about $P$ through twice the angle from the first line to the second.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An isometry $A(x) = Bx + v$ of $E^2$ where $B$ is a rotation (not the identity) is, as a map of the plane, which isometry?
options: A rotation about some point $c$, where $c$ solves $(I - B)c = v$.
questions.explanation: When $B$ is a rotation, $I - B$ is invertible, so we can solve $(I - B)c = v$. Conjugating by translation to $c$ shows $A$ is a rotation about $c$ through the same angle.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Conjugating a rotation $R$ about the origin by a translation $T : x \mapsto x + p$ gives $T \circ R \circ T^{-1}$, which is which isometry?
options: A rotation about $p$ through the same angle as $R$.
A reflection in the line through $p$.
A rotation about the origin through twice the angle.
questions.explanation: Conjugating by the translation moves the centre of rotation from the origin to $p$ while keeping the angle, so $T \circ R \circ T^{-1}$ is a rotation about $p$ through the same angle.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which of the following is an orientation preserving isometry of $E^3$ that has no counterpart in $E^2$?
options: A screw rotation: a rotation about a line followed by a translation parallel to that line.
questions.explanation: By Proposition 2.4 the orientation preserving isometries of $E^3$ are the identity, translations, rotations about a line, and screw rotations. The screw rotation has no $2$-dimensional analogue.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A rotatory reflection of $E^3$, one of the orientation reversing isometries, is which composite map?
options: A reflection followed by a translation in the plane of the mirror.
A rotation about an axis followed by reflection in a plane perpendicular to that axis.
A rotation about a line followed by a translation along it.
questions.explanation: A rotatory reflection is a rotation about an axis $\ell$ followed by reflection in a plane perpendicular to $\ell$. It is one of the orientation reversing isometries of $E^3$ in Proposition 2.4.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The reflection of $E^N$ in the hyperplane $\pi = \{x : x \cdot u = \lambda\}$, with $u$ a unit vector, is given by which formula?
questions.explanation: Reflection leaves the component perpendicular to $u$ unchanged and flips the signed offset along $u$, giving $R(x) = x - 2(x \cdot u - \lambda)u$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An isometry of $E^2$ has orthogonal part equal to the identity matrix and translation vector $v \neq 0$. It is which isometry?
questions.explanation: With $A(x) = Ix + v = x + v$ and $v \neq 0$, the map is a pure translation by $v$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Composing a rotation of $E^2$ through angle $\theta_1$ about a point with a rotation through angle $\theta_2$ about a (possibly different) point gives an isometry that is, generically, which type?
options: A rotation through $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ (or a translation if $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ is a multiple of $2\pi$).
questions.explanation: The orthogonal parts multiply, giving rotation by $\theta_1 + \theta_2$. The composite is orientation preserving, so it is a rotation by that total angle, unless the total angle is a multiple of $2\pi$, when it is a translation.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Composing two glide reflections of $E^2$ (each orientation reversing) yields an isometry of which orientation type?
options: It depends on the glide vectors and can be either.
Neither, since the result need not be an isometry.
questions.explanation: The sign $\varepsilon$ of an isometry (the determinant of its orthogonal part) is multiplicative. Two orientation reversing maps have signs $(-1)(-1) = +1$, so the composite is orientation preserving.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The square of a glide reflection of $E^2$ with nonzero glide vector $w$ along the mirror is which isometry?
options: A reflection in the same line.
A rotation about a point on the mirror.
A translation by $2w$ along the mirror line.
questions.explanation: Applying the glide reflection twice reflects twice (back to identity on the orthogonal part) and adds the glide twice, giving a translation by $2w$ parallel to the mirror. This is why a glide reflection is not of finite order.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Reflecting $E^2$ in the $x$-axis and then in the $y$-axis produces which single isometry?
options: A rotation by $\pi$ about the origin.
A reflection in the line $y = x$.
questions.explanation: The two mirror lines meet at the origin at angle $\pi/2$, so the composite is a rotation about the origin through twice that angle, namely $\pi$. Concretely $(x, y) \mapsto (-x, -y)$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which statement about how an isometry $A : x \mapsto Bx + v$ of $E^N$ acts on affine combinations is correct?
options: $A$ is linear: $A(x + y) = A(x) + A(y)$ for all $x, y$.
$A$ preserves the origin in every case.
questions.explanation: Euclidean isometries are affine maps, so they commute with affine combinations: $A(\sum \lambda_j x_j) = \sum \lambda_j A(x_j)$ when the weights sum to $1$. This is used to show a finite group of isometries fixes the centroid of an orbit.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Classify the isometry $A(x) = Bx$ of $E^2$ where $B$ is the rotation matrix through angle $\pi/2$, fixing the origin. Choose its type.
questions.solution: The orthogonal part $B$ has determinant $+1$ and is not the identity, so the map is orientation preserving and fixes the origin. It is a {{1}}.
questions.explanation: An origin-fixing isometry with determinant $+1$ orthogonal part is a rotation; here it is rotation about the origin through $\pi/2$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Classify the isometry $A(x) = Bx + v$ of $E^2$ where $B$ is reflection in the $x$-axis and $v = (3, 0)$ is parallel to that axis (nonzero). Choose its type.
questions.solution: The orthogonal part is a reflection in a line $\ell$, and the translation $v$ is parallel to $\ell$ and nonzero, so the map is a {{1}}.
questions.explanation: Reflection plus a nonzero translation parallel to the mirror is a glide reflection. If the translation had been perpendicular, it would have collapsed to a pure reflection in a shifted line.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Two reflections of $E^2$ are taken in lines that meet at a point at an angle of $30$ degrees. Give the angle, in degrees, of the rotation that results from composing them.
questions.solution: The composition of two reflections in lines meeting at angle $\alpha$ is a rotation through $2\alpha$. With $\alpha = 30$ degrees, the rotation angle is {{1}} degrees.
questions.explanation: Composing reflections in two intersecting lines gives a rotation through twice the angle between them: $2 \times 30 = 60$ degrees.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An orientation reversing isometry of $E^2$ is composed with another orientation reversing isometry of $E^2$. Using that the sign is multiplicative, give the resulting sign as $+1$ or $-1$ (enter $1$ or $-1$).
questions.solution: The sign of a composite is the product of the signs: $(-1) \times (-1)$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: The orientation sign $\varepsilon$ is a homomorphism, so $\varepsilon$ of the composite is $(-1)(-1) = 1$. The result is orientation preserving.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
